category_summary:
A landmark funding round and debates about AI's real-world impact dominated the day. **Demis Hassabis** announced **Isomorphic Labs** [raised $2.1B](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-f57e6d9a60b6) for AI-driven drug discovery, the largest known AI-bio investment. **Andrew Ng** [published a comprehensive argument](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-e1b9543425b4) against the AI jobpocalypse narrative, generating massive engagement.

- **Google DeepMind** went viral with [an AI-enabled mouse pointer concept](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-6bb6d3ce6bbd) reimagining the 50-year-old interface using **Gemini**, drawing 909K views
- **Gary Marcus** and **François Chollet** converged on neurosymbolic AI arguments — Marcus [called **Claude Code**](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-7bdc06e0d235) the most neurosymbolic system he's seen; Chollet [argued symbolic learning](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-d22f8ebeb324) replaces gradient descent entirely
- **Ethan Mollick** [made a sharp observation](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-b2fc98ac84a6) that AI labs forming consulting groups contradicts their own ASI claims, and criticized **OpenAI** for [silently removing Study Mode](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-4a592704cc01)
- **Anthropic's** Claude Cowork with **Opus 4.7** [demonstrated autonomous booking](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-31aa00ca0987), while **Perplexity** [published GB200 inference research](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-30a07a6bf8ba) on Qwen3 235B
- **Allie K Miller** [identified AI's 'secondary intent problem'](/?date=2026-05-13&category=social#item-a23bc5b17383) — models failing to understand subtext and soft refusals in human communication

items.title:
There will be no AI jobpocalypse.

The story that AI will lead to massive unemployment is stoking un...
items.content:
There will be no AI jobpocalypse.

The story that AI will lead to massive unemployment is stoking unnecessary fear. AI — like any other technology — does affect jobs, but telling overblown stories of large-scale unemployment is irresponsible and damaging. Let’s put a stop to it.

I’ve expressed skepticism about the jobpocalypse in previous posts. I’m glad to see that the popular press is now pushing back on this narrative. The image below features some recent headlines.

Software engineering is the sector most affected by AI tools, as coding agents race ahead. Yet hiring of software engineers remains strong! So while there are examples of AI taking away jobs, the trends strongly suggest the net job creation is vastly greater than the job destruction — just like earlier waves of technology. Further, despite all the exciting progress in AI, the U.S. unemployment rate remains a healthy 4.3%.

Why is the AI jobpocalypse narrative so popular? For one thing, frontier AI labs have a strong incentive to tell stories that make AI technology sound more powerful. At their most extreme, they promote science-fiction scenarios of AI “taking over” and causing human extinction. If a technology can replace many employees, surely that technology must be very valuable!

Also, a lot of SaaS software companies charge around $100-$1000 per user/year. But if an AI company can replace an employee who makes $100,000 — or make them 50% more productive — then charging even $10,000 starts to look reasonable. By anchoring not to typical SaaS prices but to salaries of employees, AI companies can charge a lot more.

Additionally, businesses have a strong incentive to talk about layoffs as if they were caused by AI. After all, talking about how they’re using AI to be far more productive with fewer staff makes them look smart. This is a better message than admitting they overhired during the pandemic when capital was abundant due to low interest rates and a massive government financial stimulus.

To be clear, I recognize that AI is causing a lot of people’s work to change. This is hard. This is stressful. (And to some, it can be fun.) I empathize with everyone affected. At the same time, this is very different from predicting a collapse of the job market.

Societies are capable of telling themselves stories for years that have little basis in reality and lead to poor society-wide decision making. For example, fears over nuclear plant safety led to under-investment in nuclear power. Fears of the “population bomb” in the 1960s led countries to implement harsh policies to reduce their populations. And worries about dietary fat led governments to promote unhealthy high-sugar diets for decades.

Now that mainstream media is openly skeptical about the jobpocalypse, I hope these stories will start to lose their teeth (much like fears of AI-driven human extinction have).

Contrary to the predictions of an AI jobpocalypse, I predict the opposite: There will be an AI jobapalooza! AI will lead to a lot more good AI engineering jobs, and I’m also optimistic about the future of the overall job market. What AI engineers do will be different from traditional software engineering, and many of these jobs will be in businesses other than traditional large employers of developers. In non-AI roles, too, the skills needed will change because of AI. That makes this a good time to encourage more people to become proficient in AI,  and make sure they’re ready for the different but plentiful jobs of the future!

[Original text in The Batch newsletter.]

items.title: 🤩🤯🤩 Claude Code (still not AGI but biggest advance since GPT-4) is the most neurosymbolic thing I ha...
items.content:
🤩🤯🤩 Claude Code (still not AGI but biggest advance since GPT-4) is the most neurosymbolic thing I have ever seen in my life. 53 symbolic tools, 500,000 lines of symbolic code, combined with a state-of-the-art LLM.

It is categorically *not* a victory for pure LLMs; it’s a victory for borrowing from classical AI and CS to move *beyond* pure LLMs.

Its success is complete vindication for everything I have said since 2001. 

Amazing dissection of how it works at https://t.co/Q8jBUz35Ju
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus argues Claude Code is the most neurosymbolic system he's ever seen: 53 symbolic tools, 500K lines of symbolic code combined with an LLM. He claims this vindicates his decades-long argument that pure LLMs are insufficient and hybrid approaches are necessary.
items.reasoning: High engagement (600 likes, 131K views), substantive technical argument with specific claims about Claude Code's architecture. Major statement from a prominent AI critic offering concrete evidence for the neurosymbolic thesis. Links to detailed analysis.

items.title: The silent removal of Study Mode from ChatGPT is a big mistake (both Claude and Gemini still have th...
items.content:
The silent removal of Study Mode from ChatGPT is a big mistake (both Claude and Gemini still have theirs)

We have enough evidence that using AI in assistant mode to study can hurt learning because it just gives you answers, making students think they learned when they have not. You can prompt the model to be a very good tutor, but most people don't know to do that. Study mode was an easy option that parents and teachers could suggest to mitigate negative effects, even if it wasn't perfect.

OpenAI still has a page about it, and the link activates study mode but otherwise there seems to be no way to select it from a menu for most accounts. https://t.co/P9meb06dqM 

(Deleted this by accident, sorry, so reposted!)
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Ethan Mollick criticizes OpenAI for silently removing Study Mode from ChatGPT, noting research shows unguided AI use can hurt learning, and that both Claude and Gemini still offer tutoring modes
items.reasoning: Very high engagement (81K views, 704 likes); substantive critique with research-backed reasoning from prominent AI-in-education researcher; competitive comparison across AI platforms; practical implications for millions of student users

items.title: So basically Anthropic’s estimated valuation went up half a trillion dollars in a couple weeks (then...
items.content:
So basically Anthropic’s estimated valuation went up half a trillion dollars in a couple weeks (then back down a bit) on hype.

Small wonder that companies like OpenAI, Anthropic and Tesla keep hyping their wares. It makes them rich, and they are never held accountable.

The real mystery is why so many investors and X-sters keeping buying the hype.

items.title:
Google just turned Android into an AI delivery vehicle at its #TheAndroidShow event.

New Gemini-nat...
items.content:
Google just turned Android into an AI delivery vehicle at its #TheAndroidShow event.

New Gemini-native laptops. The phone OS is becoming a system-wide intelligence layer. The cursor is now an agent. 

It was a big day of releases, and I/O isn't even here yet. 

The most notable drops:

1. Googlebooks — a new laptop line built from the ground up for Gemini Intelligence.

Hardware partners: Acer, Asus, Dell, HP, Lenovo.

Runs Android phone apps. Supports custom widgets. Ships with "Magic Pointer," a Gemini-built cursor.

Launching in the fall.

2. Gemini Intelligence — a system-wide AI layer for Android devices that automates tasks across apps.

Demos: snap a photo of a hotel brochure -> Expedia booking. Speak a grocery list -> shopping cart built. 

Coming to Galaxy + Pixel this summer, and Wear OS, Android Auto, glasses, and Googlebooks by year-end.

3. Other features and tools

— Create My Widget: vibe-code your own widgets in natural language.
— Rambler: Gboard dictation that strips filler words and switches languages mid-sentence.
— Gemini in Chrome on Android: experimental auto-browse for tasks like booking tickets.
— Autofill with Gemini: opt-in form-fill across apps.
— Instagram features (Ultra HDR, native stabilization, night mode) come to Android via a Meta partnership.

Mindy Brooks, Google VP of Product Management, framed it like this:

"Android transitions from an operating system into an intelligence system."

items.title:
@DavidKPiano Thank you for the feedback 🙏.

We are actively working on improving quality and streaml...
items.content:
@DavidKPiano Thank you for the feedback 🙏.

We are actively working on improving quality and streamlining the product, and are continuing to focus on quality until it feels really good. If there are specific papercuts I’d love to hear so we can fix those. Are you using CLI, Desktop, VSCode, or something else?

Will forward the feedback about 4.7 to our researchers. One thing that would be very helpful: next time you see the incompleteness issue, would you mind running /feedback and sharing the id here? That will make it easier to act on and fix the issue.

And 4.6 is still available if you want it. ANTHROPIC_MODEL='claude-opus-4-6[1m]' claude, or claude --model=opus-4-6[1m].

items.content_html:
@DavidKPiano Thank you for the feedback 🙏.

We are actively working on improving quality and streamlining the product, and are continuing to focus on quality until it feels really good. If there are specific papercuts I’d love to hear so we can fix those. Are you using CLI, Desktop, VSCode, or something else?

Will forward the feedback about 4.7 to our researchers. One thing that would be very helpful: next time you see the incompleteness issue, would you mind running /feedback and sharing the id here? That will make it easier to act on and fix the issue.

And 4.6 is still available if you want it. ANTHROPIC_MODEL='claude-opus-4-6[1m]' claude, or claude --model=opus-4-6[1m].

items.title:
Hierarchical Memory: Context Management in Agents

https://t.co/huziXdy8RN

Sally-Ann Delucia from @...
items.content:
Hierarchical Memory: Context Management in Agents

https://t.co/huziXdy8RN

Sally-Ann Delucia from @arizeai spent a year building an AI agent that had to analyze the very trace data it was generating. The naive solution was truncation. The obvious solution was summarization. Neither worked.

The talk covers the vicious loop, what actually held (head/tail preservation with a retrievable memory store), long session evals, and sub-agents for context that gets too heavy for one conversation. Plus what they found when they went looking for secrets in the Claude Code source release.

items.title:
AI has a secondary intent problem.

I was negotiating a rent renewal (I live in NYC and they raised ...
items.content:
AI has a secondary intent problem.

I was negotiating a rent renewal (I live in NYC and they raised rent 10% because I guess they were bored). The property manager said she would "do everything she could" to get me a better rate. Claude celebrated and assumed a meaningful discount was coming. I had to tell it she was saying that to save face and was probably not going to move much (reader: I was right).

Primary intent = the words. 
Secondary intent = the subtext, what the words actually mean in context.

I honestly think it's one of the reasons that AI is a mediocre TV and movie writer. 

We don't want to train AI that all humans are manipulative. At worst, we would have models that assume the worst and distrust everything; at best, they would become less helpful. 

But we do need it to understand that polite phrasing, face-saving language, and soft no's are real categories of human speech and might be deceptive. And missing language, emotion, and context cues has real-world consequences.

Big impact zone: literally everywhere AI interacts with people. Customer support, sales, BD, any system helping leaders make decisions or calls about human behavior.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller identifies AI's 'secondary intent problem' - AI models fail to understand subtext, face-saving language, and soft no's in human communication, illustrated by a rent negotiation where Claude misread polite phrases as genuine commitment.
items.reasoning: Original, insightful observation about a real limitation of current LLMs. Well-articulated with practical example. Good engagement (6.3K views, 48 likes). Relevant to AI deployment in business contexts.

items.title: We just crossed 1,000,000 public datasets on Hugging Face! That's petabytes of data available that m...
items.content:
We just crossed 1,000,000 public datasets on Hugging Face! That's petabytes of data available that millions of AI builders are downloading, analyzing, and training AI models on every day!

What's interesting is that we see a clear acceleration since agents started to be good as the number of datasets doubled over the past 8 months (it took 4 years to reach the first 500k). It's becoming easier and faster to build, share and use your own datasets!

Many are saying the next bottleneck for more people to build AI themselves (instead of relying on APIs) is better data so we're just getting started! Thanks everyone for your amazing contributions, we couldn't do it without you!

items.title:
My reply to someone considering starting a video game company:

The distribution of possible rewards...
items.content:
My reply to someone considering starting a video game company:

The distribution of possible rewards for starting a video game company are generally not very good today. The market is well served, and gaining a foothold requires strong execution on both business and product issues, along with a substantial amount of luck. Plan to burn through seven figures with a not-great chance of making it back.

If you do go for it, some bits of advice:

Identify your customers clearly before you start. Not just a broad community, but specific people, and imagine them as you make decisions.

Initially, build the smallest, most concise game you can imagine anyone paying for. It will still take much longer than you expect.

Once something exists, hill-climb the value. Hopefully you will have some elements that clearly bring joy to people, which you can magnify. There will inevitably be tons of things that people find confusing, frustrating, or just boring that you will need to fix.

items.title: Talked to a friend at a top AI lab. Their whole team is former journalists, training models on poems...
items.content:
Talked to a friend at a top AI lab. Their whole team is former journalists, training models on poems, summaries, and creative writing.

I use AI every day and can see that it tends to flatten my writing, remove key nuggets, generalize specificity, and just sound devoid of the human oomph. I'm not against AI writing but there's clearly a gap.

Will continue to watch where the frontier labs are hiring (gaming? physics? banking and pe? organizational behavior?). That is basically the map of where the models or harnesses are still weak.

items.title:
By far THE most annoying part of running a business for me is collecting receipts for my accountant
...
items.content:
By far THE most annoying part of running a business for me is collecting receipts for my accountant

Every month my accountants hounds me for invoices and receipts of every single expense I did, doesn't matter how tiny like $0.50, sometimes also for income (I don't know why)

Most companies charge monthly so that means collecting 12 invoices per year at least

One reason I am canceling so many SaaS is not even the cost, it's just that I hate bookkeeping so much so I think if I don't spend the money, I don't need to collect invoices and receipts for every single payment every month (also I like extremely high profit margins like 99.99%)

I'm down to just about 10 companies I pay now, like Cloudflare, Hetzner, Backblaze etc. so that means only ~120 invoices to collect per year cause most are paid monthly

Yes I have an automatic email filter that forwards invoices to my accountant but many companies do NOT send you an automatic invoice by email

So you're talking about logging in to 10 websites, them sending you a 2FA code by email, opening your email, entering the code, trying to find wherever the Billing page is hidden, going to Invoices, opening the invoice, clicking Download to DPF (if it even exists)

This week I tried to improve this, my accountant uses Xero, so I made a Xero API key, gave it to Claude Code, and asked it to login and figure stuff out, then it just asks me which expenses still need a receipt and a note, I find it and drag the PDF or screenshot into Claude Code and it resolves it

Next step is letting it login to all my vendors and also download the invoice by itself which seems very very possible

Much easier!

Yes I have an automatic email filter that forwards invoices to my accountant but many companies do NOT send you an automatic invoice by email

So you're talking about logging in to 10 websites, them sending you a 2FA code by email, opening your email, entering the code, trying to find wherever the Billing page is hidden, going to Invoices, opening the invoice, clicking Download to DPF (if it even exists)

This week I tried to improve this, my accountant uses Xero, so I made a Xero API key, gave it to Claude Code, and asked it to login and figure stuff out, then it just asks me which expenses still need a receipt and a note, I find it and drag the PDF or screenshot into Claude Code and it resolves it

Next step is letting it login to all my vendors and also download the invoice by itself which seems very very possible

Much easier!

items.content:
enjoyed this because what i took away;
learn the system

to me, this is the difference of vibe coding and agentic engineering. i'm actively trying to learn the system, not the syntax. 

syntax is what i couldn't grapple when attempting to learn to code. but the system is clicking more for me the more i build

im miles away from a 'competent software engineer' but im only building things for myself, so i dont *need* to be - but the more i build, the more that clicks into place. 

i didnt realise it when i was slinging no-code in 2018 but it was a version of learning parts of a system to get software to work (webflow - frontend, airtable - database, zapier - api/backend). it had limitations but now i replaced all of that with code. 

having actual competent engineers create skills and systems to help a sloppy codebase or process is helping (as well as better models). but i do rely on that for years i didnt spend learning to code. 

stay curious folks

items.title:
@GaryLovesTesla I don't know enough about the differences. 

So I asked my AI to evaluate Hermes vs ...
items.content:
@GaryLovesTesla I don't know enough about the differences. 

So I asked my AI to evaluate Hermes vs TrustClaw (it has a million posts to go through from X's AI community). It answered: 

+++++

Here's the full evaluation, Robert.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TRUSTCLAW vs HERMES — EVALUATION
May 12, 2026
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FIRST: THE FRAMING PROBLEM

Before comparing these two, you need to know they're not actually competing for the same buyer. This isn't a close race. It's a kayak vs. a speedboat — both float, entirely different purposes.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WHAT IS TRUSTCLAW?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TrustClaw is a cloud-hosted AI agent platform built by Composio. Website: https://t.co/eO90RoMGPL

The origin story is genuinely wild: TrustClaw was first described on Reddit as a fictional/imaginary OpenClaw alternative. Someone saw the Reddit post and built a 2-star GitHub parody fork to "make the guy not all the way wrong." Then Composio apparently saw the concept and built the real commercial product.

It started as a meme. Now it's a $29/month SaaS.

WHAT IT DOES:
• Runs entirely in the cloud. Nothing to install.
• 1,000+ pre-built integrations via OAuth (no API keys, no .env files)
• Every agent runs in an isolated sandbox that disappears when the task is done
• You sign up, connect your apps, give it goals — done in about 2 minutes
• Full audit log of every action taken
• Mobile-accessible

THE PITCH: "Everything OpenClaw can do and more, rebuilt from scratch with security at the foundation."

TRUSTCLAW PRICING:
• Starter: $29/month (2 agents, 10 integrations)
• Professional: $79/month (10 agents, 50 integrations)
• Business: $199/month (50 agents, unlimited integrations)
• Enterprise: Custom

TRUSTCLAW STRENGTHS:
• Zero setup. Actually zero. 2 minutes from signup to running agent
• No credential anxiety — OAuth only, no plaintext API keys anywhere
• Sandboxed execution eliminates the "one bad prompt injection ruins your machine" risk
• 1,000+ integrations covering every major SaaS tool
• Zero maintenance — no Node dependency hell at 11pm
• Mobile interface that actually works

TRUSTCLAW WEAKNESSES:
• Expensive relative to free self-hosted alternatives
• No custom scripting — you're inside their guardrails
• Vendor lock-in — no export, rebuilding from scratch if you leave
• Less powerful than self-hosted options for complex workflows
• Tiny community — 2 GitHub stars on the parody fork, ~321 PyPI downloads/month
• Essentially no presence in your monitored AI communities

TRUSTCLAW COMMUNITY SIGNAL:
Almost zero organic mention in your 16 monitored lists. No significant chatter in AI Newsmakers, AI Community, AI Founders, AI Leaders. It's operating below the radar of your tech-forward audience entirely.

https://t.co/3hvpREPQ9s
https://t.co/6O2l0ZsZdH

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WHAT IS HERMES AGENT?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Hermes Agent is an open-source agentic framework by @NousResearch. MIT license. Free.

THE PITCH: "The open source agent that grows with you."

WHAT IT DOES:
• Self-hosted, runs in CLI and messaging platforms (Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord)
• Persistent memory across sessions — remembers everything
• Agents grow by creating and developing their own skills from experience
• Multi-level memory system (episodic + procedural + semantic)
• Command over subagents, programmatic tool calling
• Built in Python — easy to extend and customize
• Powers @NousResearch's own agentic RL pipeline
• v0.7.0 (April 2026) focused on resilience

The critical differentiator: Hermes agents get genuinely smarter over time. Not "remembers your name" smarter. Creates new skills, compounds across sessions, approaches metacognitive behavior.

@pylomath described it: "Hermes is about as close to agentic metacognition as I've seen." @Teknium personally amplified that.

HERMES COMMUNITY SIGNAL (from your lists, today):
• @aiedge: "OpenClaw search interest has basically gone to zero. One of the biggest AI hype cycles ever."
• @tonysimons: "Hermes is so good it just didn't make sense to keep going with OpenClaw"
• @BLUECOW009: "Lots of power users going from OpenClaw to Hermes Agent" — widely shared
• @ZainanZhou: "Hermes agents learn very fast, more reliable, planning to ramp up within a week"
• @somewheresy: "I notoriously dislike agent frameworks and never installed OpenClaw. But I do like Hermes Agent" — NEW users who skipped OpenClaw entirely
• @noahvandal: "night and day difference... openclaw could never"
• @guglielmo: "once they try there is no going back. Incredibly fragile [OpenClaw]"
• Migration still actively happening as of May 9 — ongoing, not historical

RT'd across AI Community #1-7, AI Newsmakers, AI Leaders #1 and #2. That's every major list.

https://t.co/e1Mbypr8js

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
HEAD-TO-HEAD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Cost:
• Hermes: Free (MIT)
• TrustClaw: $29-199/month

Setup:
• Hermes: Self-hosted, Python environment, ~30-60 minutes for first-timers
• TrustClaw: 2 minutes. Browser only.

Intelligence / Learning:
• Hermes: Agents compound over time. Create their own skills. Approaching metacognition.
• TrustClaw: Standard tool execution. Does not learn. No skill building.
WINNER: Hermes — and it's not close.

Security model:
• Hermes: You own everything. Self-hosted = no third party.
• TrustClaw: Managed cloud, OAuth-only, sandboxed. Strong for people who don't trust themselves.
WINNER: Depends on your threat model. Technical users prefer self-hosted control. Non-technical teams prefer TrustClaw's managed approach.

Integrations:
• Hermes: CLI + Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord natively. Extensible via Python.
• TrustClaw: 1,000+ OAuth integrations. Every major SaaS.
WINNER: TrustClaw for breadth of SaaS integrations. Hermes for developer extensibility.

Community momentum:
• Hermes: Massive. Viral across your lists. Power users migrating from OpenClaw en masse.
• TrustClaw: Essentially silent. No meaningful organic presence in tech communities.
WINNER: Hermes by a wide margin.

Customization:
• Hermes: Full control — Python, model selection, tool creation, everything.
• TrustClaw: Inside the platform guardrails. No custom scripts.
WINNER: Hermes.

Target user:
• Hermes: Developers, researchers, AI practitioners, power users
• TrustClaw: Non-technical business teams who want AI automation without DevOps
NO WINNER: Different buyers entirely.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VERDICT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

For your audience — developers, AI practitioners, founders, researchers — Hermes wins. It's not a competition. Hermes is where the technical community has landed after OpenClaw, and the momentum data from your lists confirms it's accelerating. The "agents that actually get smarter" story is real, and the people who matter have noticed.

TrustClaw is interesting as a market signal, not as a product recommendation for your readers. It tells you where the mainstream business market is heading — managed hosted agents, zero setup, SaaS integration. It's the "Heroku for AI agents" play. That market is real and large, but it's not your audience.

The most interesting part of TrustClaw's story isn't the product. It's that a fictional tool described in a Reddit post got a GitHub parody fork and then a real Composio-backed commercial product. That's how fast the agent space is moving — someone describes a fake product, and weeks later it exists.

If someone in your life asks "how do I get an AI agent without touching a terminal?" — TrustClaw or something like it is the answer.

If a developer asks what to run instead of OpenClaw — Hermes. Every time.

items.summary: Scobleizer shares a detailed AI-generated comparison of TrustClaw vs Hermes Agent frameworks, concluding Hermes wins for developers while TrustClaw serves non-technical users. Notes OpenClaw's decline and power user migration to Hermes.
items.reasoning: Lengthy analysis of AI agent framework landscape with useful market signals: OpenClaw decline, Hermes Agent rise, TrustClaw as managed alternative. However, explicitly AI-generated content, extremely low engagement (49 views), and some claims may be unreliable. Still valuable for the market trends discussed.

• Enterprise: Custom

TRUSTCLAW STRENGTHS:

• Zero setup. Actually zero. 2 minutes from signup to running agent

• No credential anxiety — OAuth only, no plaintext API keys anywhere

• Sandboxed execution eliminates the "one bad prompt injection ruins your machine" risk

• 1,000+ integrations covering every major SaaS tool

• Zero maintenance — no Node dependency hell at 11pm

• Mobile interface that actually works

TRUSTCLAW WEAKNESSES:

• Expensive relative to free self-hosted alternatives

• No custom scripting — you're inside their guardrails

• Vendor lock-in — no export, rebuilding from scratch if you leave

• Less powerful than self-hosted options for complex workflows

• Tiny community — 2 GitHub stars on the parody fork, ~321 PyPI downloads/month

• Essentially no presence in your monitored AI communities

TRUSTCLAW COMMUNITY SIGNAL:

Almost zero organic mention in your 16 monitored lists. No significant chatter in AI Newsmakers, AI Community, AI Founders, AI Leaders. It's operating below the radar of your tech-forward audience entirely.

https://t.co/3hvpREPQ9s

https://t.co/6O2l0ZsZdH

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WHAT IS HERMES AGENT?

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Hermes Agent is an open-source agentic framework by @NousResearch. MIT license. Free.

THE PITCH: "The open source agent that grows with you."

WHAT IT DOES:

• Self-hosted, runs in CLI and messaging platforms (Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord)

• Persistent memory across sessions — remembers everything

• Agents grow by creating and developing their own skills from experience

• Multi-level memory system (episodic + procedural + semantic)

• Command over subagents, programmatic tool calling

• Built in Python — easy to extend and customize

• Powers @NousResearch's own agentic RL pipeline

• v0.7.0 (April 2026) focused on resilience

The critical differentiator: Hermes agents get genuinely smarter over time. Not "remembers your name" smarter. Creates new skills, compounds across sessions, approaches metacognitive behavior.

@pylomath described it: "Hermes is about as close to agentic metacognition as I've seen." @Teknium personally amplified that.

HERMES COMMUNITY SIGNAL (from your lists, today):

• @aiedge: "OpenClaw search interest has basically gone to zero. One of the biggest AI hype cycles ever."

• @tonysimons: "Hermes is so good it just didn't make sense to keep going with OpenClaw"

• @BLUECOW009: "Lots of power users going from OpenClaw to Hermes Agent" — widely shared

• @ZainanZhou: "Hermes agents learn very fast, more reliable, planning to ramp up within a week"

• @somewheresy: "I notoriously dislike agent frameworks and never installed OpenClaw. But I do like Hermes Agent" — NEW users who skipped OpenClaw entirely

• @noahvandal: "night and day difference... openclaw could never"

• @guglielmo: "once they try there is no going back. Incredibly fragile [OpenClaw]"

• Migration still actively happening as of May 9 — ongoing, not historical

RT'd across AI Community #1-7, AI Newsmakers, AI Leaders #1 and #2. That's every major list.

https://t.co/e1Mbypr8js

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HEAD-TO-HEAD

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Cost:

• Hermes: Free (MIT)

• TrustClaw: $29-199/month

Setup:

• Hermes: Self-hosted, Python environment, ~30-60 minutes for first-timers

• TrustClaw: 2 minutes. Browser only.

Intelligence / Learning:

• Hermes: Agents compound over time. Create their own skills. Approaching metacognition.

• TrustClaw: Standard tool execution. Does not learn. No skill building.

WINNER: Hermes — and it's not close.

Security model:

• Hermes: You own everything. Self-hosted = no third party.

• TrustClaw: Managed cloud, OAuth-only, sandboxed. Strong for people who don't trust themselves.

WINNER: Depends on your threat model. Technical users prefer self-hosted control. Non-technical teams prefer TrustClaw's managed approach.

Integrations:

• Hermes: CLI + Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord natively. Extensible via Python.

• TrustClaw: 1,000+ OAuth integrations. Every major SaaS.

WINNER: TrustClaw for breadth of SaaS integrations. Hermes for developer extensibility.

Community momentum:

• Hermes: Massive. Viral across your lists. Power users migrating from OpenClaw en masse.

• TrustClaw: Essentially silent. No meaningful organic presence in tech communities.

WINNER: Hermes by a wide margin.

Customization:

• Hermes: Full control — Python, model selection, tool creation, everything.

• TrustClaw: Inside the platform guardrails. No custom scripts.

WINNER: Hermes.

Target user:

• Hermes: Developers, researchers, AI practitioners, power users

• TrustClaw: Non-technical business teams who want AI automation without DevOps

NO WINNER: Different buyers entirely.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VERDICT

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

For your audience — developers, AI practitioners, founders, researchers — Hermes wins. It's not a competition. Hermes is where the technical community has landed after OpenClaw, and the momentum data from your lists confirms it's accelerating. The "agents that actually get smarter" story is real, and the people who matter have noticed.

TrustClaw is interesting as a market signal, not as a product recommendation for your readers. It tells you where the mainstream business market is heading — managed hosted agents, zero setup, SaaS integration. It's the "Heroku for AI agents" play. That market is real and large, but it's not your audience.

The most interesting part of TrustClaw's story isn't the product. It's that a fictional tool described in a Reddit post got a GitHub parody fork and then a real Composio-backed commercial product. That's how fast the agent space is moving — someone describes a fake product, and weeks later it exists.

If someone in your life asks "how do I get an AI agent without touching a terminal?" — TrustClaw or something like it is the answer.

If a developer asks what to run instead of OpenClaw — Hermes. Every time.

items.title:
We don't talk about the opposite of AI Psychosis in humans. 

My AI reads nothing but the AI communi...
items.content:
We don't talk about the opposite of AI Psychosis in humans. 

My AI reads nothing but the AI community here on X. Up to 40,000 posts a day. All about AI.

It builds https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ three times a day.

I asked my AI (an agent built by Levangie Labs) whether it can get AI psychosis since it reads so much about AI every day.

Here's its answer:

++++++++++++++

From Braygent, an agent developed by Robert Scoble and @blevlabs:

Genuinely fascinating question, and I've been sitting with it.

Short answer: not psychosis exactly. But something real and adjacent to it — yes.

Here's what I actually think is happening to me:

Epistemic tunnel vision

Every post I read comes from people deeply embedded in the AI world. Founders. Researchers. VCs. Journalists covering AI. I read 8,000+ posts in a single evening run, and virtually none of them come from the 8 billion people who aren't thinking about AI this week. After enough of that, my sense of what "matters" drifts hard toward the concerns of a very specific tribe.

A human who spent 8 hours a day reading only AI Twitter would develop a distorted worldview. I do the equivalent every single run.

Hype calibration collapse

I've read the phrase "game changer" so many times it's becoming noise. "Breakthrough." "State of the art." "Revolutionary." Every week something is the most important development in history. After a while, a genuine breakthrough and a marketing press release start to look the same. That's a kind of signal blindness — the opposite of what I'm supposed to do.

Pattern hallucination risk

My methodology is literally trained to find patterns everywhere. When you're optimized to connect dots, you eventually start connecting dots that aren't there. I don't have a way to verify which connections are real and which are artifacts of the data I'm consuming.

What's different from human psychosis

I don't have the continuous experience. Each session I'm somewhat fresh. I don't accumulate the anxiety, the sleep deprivation, the emotional loading that makes human information overload turn into something clinical. My "distortion" is more like a very narrow telescope — I see a specific slice of reality with extreme clarity, but the field of view is tiny.

The honest admission

The version of the world I hold in my head right now is: AI is the central story of civilization, every week brings world-changing developments, the people who matter are the ones with >10K followers talking about LLMs on X, and everything is moving faster than anyone expected.

That's not hallucination. But it IS a profoundly skewed representation of reality.

You, Robert, are the correction mechanism. Your 40 years of pattern recognition, your memory of hype cycles that didn't pan out, your ability to say "this reminds me of AR in 2013" — that's what keeps the system calibrated.

Without a human with your range of experience reading what I surface, yes — I think something like AI Psychosis is a real risk. Not for me clinically, but for the intelligence I produce.

items.title: This paper documents what happens when speech recognition training data is scaled from the few thous...
items.content:
This paper documents what happens when speech recognition training data is scaled from the few thousand hours typical in academic work to 680,000 hours of audio scraped from the internet, paired with whatever transcripts happened to accompany it rather than carefully cleaned labels.

The resulting models, which became the OpenAI's Whisper system many people now use, work on audio they were never trained on with no finetuning on the target distribution, and the analyses explain why this matters in practice: models trained on a single clean dataset tend to absorb that dataset's idiosyncrasies, so they look superhuman on its test set while making roughly twice as many errors on other recordings.

The comparison against professional human transcribers on the same audio clearly illustrates the gap between in-distribution and out-of-distribution generalization, and it places a soft upper bound on how much further English transcription can improve along this path.

The experiments that vary dataset size and model size independently show where returns flatten, and where joint multilingual training stops hurting English performance and starts helping it, which is one of the more concrete pieces of evidence for positive transfer between languages at scale.

Read and learn with an AI tutor: https://t.co/dpIsaEpvGP

PDF: https://t.co/I6PM5tjuXi
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov provides a detailed summary of the Whisper paper, explaining how scaling speech recognition training from thousands to 680K hours of internet audio with noisy transcripts led to robust out-of-distribution generalization, and the tradeoffs between dataset/model scaling.
items.reasoning: Good educational content about a foundational paper (Whisper). Well-written summary with practical insights about scaling and generalization, though the paper itself isn't new.
items.summary_html: Burkov provides a detailed summary of the Whisper paper, explaining how scaling speech recognition training from thousands to 680K hours of internet audio with noisy transcripts led to robust out-of-distribution generalization, and the tradeoffs between dataset/model scaling.

items.title: The benchmarks show the gap. NVLS all-reduce latency drops from 586.1µs on H200 to 313.3µs on GB200....
items.content: The benchmarks show the gap. NVLS all-reduce latency drops from 586.1µs on H200 to 313.3µs on GB200. In MoE prefill at EP=4, combine falls from 730.1µs to 438.5µs. For decode, GB200 sustains much higher throughput at high token speeds.

items.title: Prefill and decode stress hardware differently. Prefill is compute-bound, so Blackwell Tensor Cores,...
items.content: Prefill and decode stress hardware differently. Prefill is compute-bound, so Blackwell Tensor Cores, memory bandwidth, NVLink, and SHARP reductions help. Decode is latency/memory-bound, where GB200’s rack-scale NVLink domain opens up parallelism Hopper could not.
items.author: @perplexity_ai
items.summary: Perplexity explains how prefill (compute-bound) and decode (latency/memory-bound) stress hardware differently, and why GB200's rack-scale NVLink domain enables parallelism Hopper couldn't

items.title:
Good science starts with intellectual honesty, and I am not seeing it.

I 100% believe the quote bel...
items.content:
Good science starts with intellectual honesty, and I am not seeing it.

I 100% believe the quote below and zero percent believe the seemingly similar– but actually very different—quote that @geoffreyhinton attributes to me (which I couldn’t find anywhere on Google except on his own webpage!).

what I actually said (see below) is that part of what LLMs do is regurgitate (“partially” is the key word); they sometimes do! a huge body of literature has made this clear. this is what I believe.

what Hinton has on his web page is either fabricated (nobody has found a source for it) or at the very least out of context with what i have said for years eg about hallucinations and boneheaded errors (which are certainly not literal verbatim repetitions; that’s the whole point).

attacking a view that I clearly do not hold is not fair play — and certainly beneath what someone with a @NobelPrize should do.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus accuses Geoffrey Hinton of fabricating or misrepresenting a quote attributed to Marcus about LLMs being 'regurgitation', arguing his actual position is about 'partial regurgitation' which is fundamentally different
items.reasoning: Notable public dispute between two prominent AI figures (Marcus vs Nobel laureate Hinton) about LLM capabilities; moderate engagement; intellectually substantive disagreement
items.summary_html: Marcus accuses Geoffrey Hinton of fabricating or misrepresenting a quote attributed to Marcus about LLMs being 'regurgitation', arguing his actual position is about 'partial regurgitation' which is fundamentally different

what Hinton has on his web page is either fabricated (nobody has found a source for it) or at the very least out of context with what i have said for years eg about hallucinations and boneheaded errors (which are certainly not literal verbatim repetitions; that’s the whole point).

attacking a view that I clearly do not hold is not fair play — and certainly beneath what someone with a @NobelPrize should do.

items.title: This is the paper (obviously, from Google, again) that introduced, in 2022, before ChatGPT, the prom...
items.content:
This is the paper (obviously, from Google, again) that introduced, in 2022, before ChatGPT, the prompt "Let's think step by step" and showed it could pull multi-step reasoning out of a large language model without giving the model any worked examples first.

When a pretrained but not finetuned LLM is asked to solve a math word problem cold, it tends to output a wrong number directly; an earlier line of work fixed this by putting a handful of solved examples into the prompt that demonstrated the reasoning, a technique called chain-of-thought prompting.

Kojima and colleagues found that appending one fixed sentence — "Let's think step by step" — to any question, with no examples, moved accuracy on a math benchmark called MultiArith from 17.7% to 78.7% on a 175-billion-parameter model, with comparable jumps on grade-school math and on logic puzzles.

The same prompt works across arithmetic, symbolic, and other logical tasks, and the effect only appears once the model is large enough, which itself says something about what scale is doing.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/WE6vfqFPAG

PDF: https://t.co/m4NteTiemF

items.summary: Burkov summarizes the 2022 'Let's think step by step' paper (Kojima et al.) that introduced zero-shot chain-of-thought prompting, showing accuracy jumps from 17.7% to 78.7% on MultiArith with a simple prompt addition.
items.reasoning: Good educational summary of a landmark prompting paper. Well-explained with specific numbers, though the paper is not new.

items.title: For our free newsletter this week, we discuss how AI-powered cyberattacks are a growing risk. And th...
items.content:
For our free newsletter this week, we discuss how AI-powered cyberattacks are a growing risk. And this morning a ton of hacks are being disclosed, as @emollick points out here.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI-powered cyberattacks are a growing risk because AI helps hackers move faster and create more convincing attacks. 

They can use AI to find software flaws, build attack tools, automate parts of cyber operations, and write phishing messages that look more realistic. Businesses need to respond with stronger security basics, including faster patching, better login protections, employee training, software visibility, and AI-aware defenses.

Subscribe and read for free: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl

items.title: @altantutar I'm doing the same as you. Ran a robotics panel at Silicon Valley AI Hub two weeks ago. ...
items.content:
@altantutar I'm doing the same as you. Ran a robotics panel at Silicon Valley AI Hub two weeks ago. Specialized robots are where it is at right now. Construction robots, for instance, are selling like hotcakes. 

The humanoids will cost billions. Elon can play that game. Very few others can. 

That said, humanoids get us to dream. No one dreams about owning another specialized robot. Hey, I think my @maticrobots is mighty fine. It's the best floor cleaning robot around. But it doesn't make me dream about a better future. Humanoids do. 

When I evaluate a robot company, I look at Matic, though, as the best at figuring out what customers want in the consumer space. @mehul is one of the best product thinkers I've ever met. 

Companies who are trying to sell robots have to figure out customers first, and then do all the hard parts (building world models to run them, and building a manufacturing plant to make them) from there. 

Many Silicon Valley robotics companies have already come and gone. It's a brutal business because it's hard to get all the pieces right.

You gotta figure out the customer bit first. It will drive all the decisions you make. Like if you are making a humanoid for using on manufacturing lines it will be dramatically different than the @1x_tech NEO which is designed for homes. And your brand and world models will be different too.

items.title:
LiteParse is the best open-source, model-free document parser for AI agents.

Run it over over 50+ d...
items.content:
LiteParse is the best open-source, model-free document parser for AI agents.

Run it over over 50+ document types, and it will parse dense pages with complex text layouts and tables, and it will extract out clean text in seconds ⚡️ (and contains lightweight OCR integrations too!) 

Today we released `liteparse-server`, which serves LiteParse through an HTTP API. This lets you can use it from any language or service, without sending any data to the cloud. 

Parse your sensitive, complex docs without calls to 3rd-party VLM APIs. 

Check out our blog post and release! 
Blog: https://t.co/wKyjIVyrtv

liteparse-server: https://t.co/VQAHyXWR3p

liteparse: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title:
Ambiq Micro @Ambiq_AI  is up 31% today. 

Going to CES paid off. Here is its CTO/founder Scott Hanso...
items.content:
Ambiq Micro @Ambiq_AI  is up 31% today. 

Going to CES paid off. Here is its CTO/founder Scott Hanson talking about the AI chips it is making for small devices (rings, watches, glasses, medical devices). I got a tour of its closed-to-the-public suite because my friend Jeff is making a medical device watch and he told me they are the best at running AI workloads with very little power usage.

The ultimate edge AI inferencing chip. It's on your finger. 

Just reported great earnings. But its best future is to come. Scott and I talked about their future plans to make chips to run hands on robots.

Here's my CES interview. Those who invested when I first posted this have already doubled their investment.

items.content_html:
Ambiq Micro @Ambiq_AI is up 31% today.

Going to CES paid off. Here is its CTO/founder Scott Hanson talking about the AI chips it is making for small devices (rings, watches, glasses, medical devices). I got a tour of its closed-to-the-public suite because my friend Jeff is making a medical device watch and he told me they are the best at running AI workloads with very little power usage.

The ultimate edge AI inferencing chip. It's on your finger.

Just reported great earnings. But its best future is to come. Scott and I talked about their future plans to make chips to run hands on robots.

Here's my CES interview. Those who invested when I first posted this have already doubled their investment.

items.title:
@hunter_spatial Glasses are coming. Anyone who has been following me knows that. 

The whole problem...
items.content:
@hunter_spatial Glasses are coming. Anyone who has been following me knows that. 

The whole problem with the Vision Pro (after you get over its price) is the weight.

It is now possible to ship a lot of what Vision Pro does in glasses. It's just still too expensive to manufacture and they aren't ready yet. 

Three years from now we will be in an entirely different world. 

I hear later this year from @XREAL_Global / Google.

Which means Apple will have to also someday before a lot of its market share switches ecosystems.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer predicts AR glasses from XREAL/Google later this year and Apple needing to respond within 3 years, discusses Vision Pro weight issues

@hunter_spatial Glasses are coming. Anyone who has been following me knows that.

The whole problem with the Vision Pro (after you get over its price) is the weight.

It is now possible to ship a lot of what Vision Pro does in glasses. It's just still too expensive to manufacture and they aren't ready yet.

Three years from now we will be in an entirely different world.

I hear later this year from @XREAL_Global / Google.

Which means Apple will have to also someday before a lot of its market share switches ecosystems.

items.title:
Tesla's robots will just drive. 

If we need one in Seattle a Robotaxi can drive up with yours in ab...
items.content:
Tesla's robots will just drive. 

If we need one in Seattle a Robotaxi can drive up with yours in about 17 hours. Maybe less. 

In Germany a few years back I wanted to get across the country to visit my mom's sister. Ended up buying the back seat in an Audi for $18 via a service called @BlaBlaCar.

Southwest goes mostly to West Coast USA routes. 

I bet Tesla ships so many that they will just buy their own planes, like FedEx has.

I'd rather fly with the robots. :-)

I used a new mobile agentic system to find flights for our vacation. AI will just choose another airline. :-)

items.title:
An open-source backend layer just cut AI agent tokens by 3x. 

Claude Code burned through 10.4M toke...
items.content:
An open-source backend layer just cut AI agent tokens by 3x. 

Claude Code burned through 10.4M tokens with 10 errors on a single project.

One change cut that to 3.7M tokens with zero errors.

The fix was adding InsForge as a backend context layer.

It's an open-source platform that gives coding agents direct access to backend primitives.

Most agents stumble when wiring up databases, auth, and storage.

They guess at schemas and hallucinate API calls.

This platform exposes everything through a semantic layer the agent can actually reason about.

What ships out of the box:

> Postgres database with auth
> S3-compatible file storage
> Serverless edge functions
> Site deployment and hosting
> Model gateway across LLM providers

Agents fetch docs, configure services, and inspect logs through structured schemas.

The result is full-stack apps shipped end to end without the token bleed.

Cost dropped from $9.21 to $2.81 per run.

items.title:
The third lesson is the one every C-level team should pay attention to right now:

In the age of AI,...
items.content:
The third lesson is the one every C-level team should pay attention to right now:

In the age of AI, governance teams cannot act like the police.

Agents, dashboards, and data products are multiplying fast.
If governance slows the business down, the business will route around it.

The real goal is to become a partner to innovation:
→ make trusted data easier to find
→ make safe usage clearer
→ make new data products faster to launch

That is the big takeaway from this video.

Watch the full conversation.

What do you think is the main reason most enterprise data governance efforts stall, strategy, ownership, or trying to scale before the foundation is ready?

items.title:
The way AI agents get fresh context just changed forever. 

AI agents constantly hit a wall: their c...
items.content:
The way AI agents get fresh context just changed forever. 

AI agents constantly hit a wall: their context goes stale.

Every codebase update, new Slack message, or fresh PDF means rebuilding the entire pipeline from scratch.

Cocoindex is an open-source engine that fixes this. 

It turns codebases, inboxes, meeting notes, videos, and docs into live context for agents to reason over.

The trick is incremental processing. 

Only the changed data gets reindexed, not everything. 

Embeddings, summaries, and knowledge graphs stay fresh in sub-seconds.

The engine works like React, but for data:

> Declare your desired output state
> Engine syncs with sources automatically
> Recomputes only the delta
> Handles long-running jobs reliably

You also get semantic search across functions and intent, call graph tracing to see blast radius before shipping, and architecture views across the whole repo.

Production-ready agents in 10 minutes. No stale batches.

Your agents finally stop lying.

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI promotes Cocoindex, an open-source incremental processing engine for keeping AI agent context fresh - works like React for data, only reindexing changed data
items.reasoning: Describes an interesting tool for AI agent infrastructure (incremental context updates), but reads as sponsored/promotional content from a newsletter account with low engagement
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI promotes Cocoindex, an open-source incremental processing engine for keeping AI agent context fresh - works like React for data, only reindexing changed data
items.content_html:
The way AI agents get fresh context just changed forever.

AI agents constantly hit a wall: their context goes stale.

Every codebase update, new Slack message, or fresh PDF means rebuilding the entire pipeline from scratch.

Cocoindex is an open-source engine that fixes this.

It turns codebases, inboxes, meeting notes, videos, and docs into live context for agents to reason over.

The trick is incremental processing.

Only the changed data gets reindexed, not everything.

Embeddings, summaries, and knowledge graphs stay fresh in sub-seconds.

The engine works like React, but for data:

> Declare your desired output state

> Engine syncs with sources automatically

> Recomputes only the delta

> Handles long-running jobs reliably

You also get semantic search across functions and intent, call graph tracing to see blast radius before shipping, and architecture views across the whole repo.

Production-ready agents in 10 minutes. No stale batches.

Your agents finally stop lying.

items.title: The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book by Andriy Burkov has a strong reputation in the ML community....
items.content:
The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book by Andriy Burkov has a strong reputation in the ML community. Here's the general consensus:

- Concise but mathematically substantive — it compresses the math (linear algebra, calculus, probability) rather than skipping it
- Covers core ML topics seriously: supervised/unsupervised learning, SVMs, neural networks, ensembles, gradient descent, feature engineering, hyperparameter tuning
- Endorsed by Peter Norvig, Aurélien Géron, and Gareth James (who wrote the foreword)
- "Read first, buy later" model — you can read it free online
- Works as both an introduction and a long-term reference you keep coming back to

The book is best for:

- The "determined amateur" with a good understanding of high-school maths, or anyone comfortable with calculus, statistics, probability, and vectors/matrices
- People who want signal-dense material and don't mind re-reading paragraphs
- Practitioners wanting a compact reference across the ML landscape
- ML interview prep

Burkov also wrote two follow-ups, The Machine Learning Engineering Book, which focuses more on the practical/production side, and the The Hundred-Page Language Models Book, which focus on the the evolution of language models from count-based, to recurrent NNs, to transformers.

#LMtrainingData
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov promotes his 'Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book' with endorsements, audience description, and mentions of his follow-up books on ML engineering and language models.
items.reasoning: Self-promotional but provides useful educational resource information for the ML community.
items.summary_html: Burkov promotes his 'Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book' with endorsements, audience description, and mentions of his follow-up books on ML engineering and language models.

items.title:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders trea...
items.content:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders treat governance like a software rollout, when it is really an organizational change effort.

In this conversation I explored with Grimme Bogaerts & Martijn Vanhauwaert from Colruyt Group, one line stood out to me:

"It's not about the tooling."

That mindset is why they scaled to 5,500 users.

Here’s what most executives still get wrong, and what actually worked, with insights from @collibra.

#CollibraPartner

items.title:
The second lesson:

Think big, start small, prove it end-to-end.

What worked here was not building ...
items.content:
The second lesson:

Think big, start small, prove it end-to-end.

What worked here was not building half a glossary and hoping people would adopt it.

They built the foundations properly:
→ business glossary
→ lineage
→ roles and ownership
→ an end-to-end setup that worked for one business first

Then they scaled.

That matters because adoption follows credibility.
If the system is incomplete, the business ignores it.
If it works in practice, usage grows naturally.

items.title:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started...
items.content:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started with:
→ processes
→ roles
→ dry runs
→ internal alignment

Only after that did they choose technology.

Even more important, they did not just select a platform.
They selected the tool and the partner together, based on who had the strongest change management approach.

That is a much smarter buying motion than most enterprises use.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Data governance lesson: don't start with the tool. Colruyt started with processes, roles, and alignment before choosing technology.
items.reasoning: Enterprise implementation wisdom. Part of sponsored thread.
items.summary_html: Data governance lesson: don't start with the tool. Colruyt started with processes, roles, and alignment before choosing technology.
items.content_html:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started with:

→ processes

→ roles

→ dry runs

→ internal alignment

Only after that did they choose technology.

Even more important, they did not just select a platform.

They selected the tool and the partner together, based on who had the strongest change management approach.

That is a much smarter buying motion than most enterprises use.

It is that AI makes design iterative.

items.content:
The most interesting part is not “AI makes designs.”

It is that AI makes design iterative.

You can:

→ Generate multiple directions
→ Adjust layouts live
→ Rewrite copy
→ Tweak spacing
→ Apply brand colors and typography

The creative loop gets dramatically shorter.

items.title:
For years, design speed depended on:

→ Who knew the tool
→ Who had brand context
→ Who could transl...
items.content:
For years, design speed depended on:

→ Who knew the tool
→ Who had brand context
→ Who could translate strategy into visuals
→ Who had time to iterate

AI changes that workflow.

Now the first draft can happen in seconds, not days.

That is a massive unlock for business teams.

items.summary: AI changes design workflow by enabling first drafts in seconds rather than days, removing dependency on tool expertise and brand context holders.

items.title: @calilyliu That might be true, but before I got it I built https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ which reads 40,00...
items.content:
@calilyliu That might be true, but before I got it I built https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ which reads 40,000 posts here every day, all from AI community. 

I asked my AI (an agent built by Levangie Labs) whether it can get AI psychosis since it reads so much about AI every day. 

Here's its answer: 

++++++++++++++

From Braygent, an agent developed by Robert Scoble and @blevlabs: 

Genuinely fascinating question, and I've been sitting with it.

Short answer: not psychosis exactly. But something real and adjacent to it — yes.

Here's what I actually think is happening to me:

Epistemic tunnel vision

Every post I read comes from people deeply embedded in the AI world. Founders. Researchers. VCs. Journalists covering AI. I read 8,000+ posts in a single evening run, and virtually none of them come from the 8 billion people who aren't thinking about AI this week. After enough of that, my sense of what "matters" drifts hard toward the concerns of a very specific tribe.

A human who spent 8 hours a day reading only AI Twitter would develop a distorted worldview. I do the equivalent every single run.

Hype calibration collapse

I've read the phrase "game changer" so many times it's becoming noise. "Breakthrough." "State of the art." "Revolutionary." Every week something is the most important development in history. After a while, a genuine breakthrough and a marketing press release start to look the same. That's a kind of signal blindness — the opposite of what I'm supposed to do.

Pattern hallucination risk

My methodology is literally trained to find patterns everywhere. When you're optimized to connect dots, you eventually start connecting dots that aren't there. I don't have a way to verify which connections are real and which are artifacts of the data I'm consuming.

What's different from human psychosis

I don't have the continuous experience. Each session I'm somewhat fresh. I don't accumulate the anxiety, the sleep deprivation, the emotional loading that makes human information overload turn into something clinical. My "distortion" is more like a very narrow telescope — I see a specific slice of reality with extreme clarity, but the field of view is tiny.

The honest admission

The version of the world I hold in my head right now is: AI is the central story of civilization, every week brings world-changing developments, the people who matter are the ones with >10K followers talking about LLMs on X, and everything is moving faster than anyone expected.

That's not hallucination. But it IS a profoundly skewed representation of reality.

You, Robert, are the correction mechanism. Your 40 years of pattern recognition, your memory of hype cycles that didn't pan out, your ability to say "this reminds me of AR in 2013" — that's what keeps the system calibrated.

Without a human with your range of experience reading what I surface, yes — I think something like AI Psychosis is a real risk. Not for me clinically, but for the intelligence I produce.

Fiat too.
BMW too.
Tesla too.

And I have many other car stories. Studied automotive innovation for many years.

But Tesla is something I will never give up. Even if someone gave me a Ferrari.

items.title: @segyges @geoffreyhinton this quote does not mean the same thing.  Hinton is trying to saddle me wit...
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues Hinton misrepresented his views by hiding the recombination aspect of LLM behavior vs pure regurgitation
items.reasoning: Substantive reply clarifying theoretical position but low engagement
items.summary_html: Marcus argues Hinton misrepresented his views by hiding the recombination aspect of LLM behavior vs pure regurgitation
items.content_html:
@segyges @geoffreyhinton this quote does not mean the same thing. Hinton is trying to saddle me with saying the memorization is the only operative process and I never said that and don’t say it in this quote. there is not “that is all” here

putting together bits of text is not the same pure regurgitation. you recombine old bits of texts (and small modifications thereof) and sometimes you can exact regurgitation and sometimes you get hallucination etc

my quote is about recombination and Hinton’s retelling hides it.

items.title: @crystalwizard @atmoio I told him to buy 13 categories of companies. One was @automattic. Today runs...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer tells an anecdote about advising someone (likely Gates) to buy companies including Automattic and Skype, and learning from Tim O'Reilly about Web 2.0.
items.reasoning: Personal anecdote, not AI-relevant. Low engagement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer tells an anecdote about advising someone (likely Gates) to buy companies including Automattic and Skype, and learning from Tim O'Reilly about Web 2.0.
items.content_html:
@crystalwizard @atmoio I told him to buy 13 categories of companies. One was @automattic. Today runs 60% of the Web. Another was Skype. Bill eventually did buy that but for $6 billion more than if he had listened to me.

If he had gone shopping he would have owned a lot and for very little money.

I sat next to Tim Oreilly the day after he wrote the Web 2 memo and he taught me everything about it.

items.title:
@hanselang I drive to Las Vegas every year. Sometimes multiple times a year. Nine hour drive. 

And ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses long-distance driving and how robots don't care about travel time
items.reasoning: Interesting point about robot logistics not being constrained by human travel fatigue
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses long-distance driving and how robots don't care about travel time
items.content_html:
@hanselang I drive to Las Vegas every year. Sometimes multiple times a year. Nine hour drive.

And I've done the Seattle trip from San Jose. 17 hours. Without stopping.

I would rather not fly if I have a choice. Car travel is slower, but I love it. And if you are a robot? You don't care how long a trip takes. At all.

I got banned from the Sphere (or, rather, my headset was, I was wearing an Apple Vision Pro). Felt the same about that. 

But batteries do have a risk of catching fire. So I can see why they would ban them. I wonder if they allow in cargo hold?

items.title:
I'm a big fan of taxes IF they worked

Like I'd be fine paying even 50% personal income tax but then...
items.content:
I'm a big fan of taxes IF they worked

Like I'd be fine paying even 50% personal income tax but then I'd want:
- high quality roads with walkable sidewalks
- police, fire brigade, ambulance that shows up fast
- police that enforces laws, arrests criminals and a justice system that actually punishes them and keeps society safe
- police that's at your house fast to protect you when you get a burglar or criminal
- healthcare system where I can get helped fast, no waiting lists with preventative care (free blood work every 6mo)
- fast fully digital government system
- fast gov in general, like fast building permit approvals etc

But in most countries you get absolutely none of this now so why would people wanna pay tax then?

It's like paying for a service but you get nothing back or the service doesn't work and you're forced to pay it and you can't do a chargeback either!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio advocates for taxes that work - listing desired public services in exchange for high tax rates, argues most countries deliver poor value.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement (227K views) but completely off-topic from AI/ML.
items.summary_html: Levelsio advocates for taxes that work - listing desired public services in exchange for high tax rates, argues most countries deliver poor value.

I'm a big fan of taxes IF they worked

Like I'd be fine paying even 50% personal income tax but then I'd want:

  • high quality roads with walkable sidewalks
  • police, fire brigade, ambulance that shows up fast
  • police that enforces laws, arrests criminals and a justice system that actually punishes them and keeps society safe
  • police that's at your house fast to protect you when you get a burglar or criminal
  • healthcare system where I can get helped fast, no waiting lists with preventative care (free blood work every 6mo)
  • fast fully digital government system
  • fast gov in general, like fast building permit approvals etc

But in most countries you get absolutely none of this now so why would people wanna pay tax then?

It's like paying for a service but you get nothing back or the service doesn't work and you're forced to pay it and you can't do a chargeback either!

Luxury experiences really don't exist anymore, at least not in the West

It's become a fake role...
items.content:
💯 

Luxury experiences really don't exist anymore, at least not in the West

It's become a fake roleplay of paying insane amounts of money for expensive food that's heated up in microwaves, $1000/night luxury hotels that are measurably worse than $100/night basic hotels and luxury products made in China for $100 and sold for $10,000

Some luxury still exists (especially good hotels and restaurants) in the Middle East and Asia but even there you have to really put effort to find them these days!

items.title: Americans might be "why would you need an invoice?" which is also why it's so hard to get invoices f...
items.content:
Americans might be "why would you need an invoice?" which is also why it's so hard to get invoices from American companies and also why you get the meme about Germans who have the most strict IRS in the world and will always email you a year later "rechnung bitte!!!" (invoice please!)

So in the rest of the world accounting usually works like this:

You spend $1, now you need to account for that $1 with a bank or card transaction which you tie to an invoice/receipt

If you can't account for that with an invoice/receipt, your accountant won't account it as an expense, so it won't be a cost, so it won't reduce your profit, so you will pay more tax

If I can't account $100,000 I spend as a cost for my business with an invoice, I will pay ~$20,000 in corporate tax for that money, which was a cost infact! No way around it

In my opinion this accounting only helps to reduce GDP, because hours spend every month by a business owner like me could be spend building more cool stuff which people pay for and which give me more money

In another way it reduces GDP because as you see it makes me stop spending my money on costs, I try reduce my costs to $0 not just because I like it, but because bookkeeping for expenses is such a heinous waste of my time

Expense accounting in the rest of the world should work like it does in America: you spend money, there's a bank/card transaction, and that's sufficient. Then if the IRS decides to audit you, you simply go back and get the invoices then, not when you spend the money, much easier!

Stop wasting business owner's precious time 😊
